content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To    :   File                                                Date  :   May 20, 1985
                Re: M-85-099


From  :  FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
         Bob Leidign

Subject:  Informal Assistance Advice


         Pursuant to discussions with Roger Brown, Barbara Milman,
Kathryn Donovan and Diane Fishburn, I advised John Frederick,
City Attorney for Manteca that if it was absolutely necessary for
them to seek a four-vote decision on the extension of the interim
ordinance tonight at the council meeting that the councilmember
in question could participate without fear of enforcement action
on the part of the FPPC.  He will proceed in that fashion only if
it is absolutely necessary to take that step.  He will accept
this informal assistance in lieu of an advice letter with the
understanding that I would have this memorandum prepared and a
copy transmitted to tne Enforcement Division.

         The basis for this advice is the application of 87101 and
18701 -- legally required participation -- to the circumstances
set forth in the attached letter and the fact that the
hospitalized councilmember had a heart attack and her doctor will
not permit her to participate in any way whatsoever.

REL:nwm

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement
(916) 322-5662        322-5660           322-5901            322-6441


May 2, 1985


John A. Frederick
Attorney at Law
Manteca City Attorney
146 North Grant Street
Manteca, CA 95336

Re: A-85-099

Dear Mr. Frederick:

Your letter requesting advice under the Political
Reform Act has been referred to Kathryn Donovan, an
attorney in the Legal Division of the Fair Political
Practices Commission. If you have any questions about your
advice request, you may contact this attorney directly at
(916) 322-5901.

We try to answer all advice requests promptly.
Therefore, unless your request poses particularly complex
legal questions, or unless more information is needed to
answer your request, you should expect a response within 21
working days.

Very truly yours,

Barbara A. Milman
General Counsel

BAM:plh

CITY ATTORNEY
146 North Grant Street
Manteca, California 95336
209-823-1112

[illegible]

April 30, 1985

Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, CA    95814

Re:  Building Moratorium
     Our File No.  226-85-202

Dear Sir/Madam:

     This office is the contract city attorney for the City
of Manteca, a general law city.  Our governing body consists
of five councilmembers, including a mayor who has a vote on
the council equal to the other four members.

     Due to a recent sewage problem, our council adopted an
urgency ordinance (No. 710) pursuant to Government Code
Section 65858, which ordinance placed a halt on the issuance
of all building permits requiring sewer connections.  To
maintain the moratorium until a non-emergency ordinance and
allocation system can be adopted, it is necessary to extend
Ordinance No. 710 beyond the initial forty-five day period
provided for in § 65858.

     Pursuant to subdivision (a) of § 65858 any extension of
Ordinance No. 710 requires a four-fifths affirmative vote of
the council.  One of the councilmembers is a real estate
developer with projects subject to the moratorium; he has
declared the conflict and stepped down.  Since the adoption
of Ordinance No. 710, another councilmember has become
seriously ill and is hospitalized in the intensive care unit.
She is not expected to recover in time to vote on the extension
of the Ordinance.

     Our city is now confronted with a problem - to stop the
moratorium from lapsing by operation of law a four-fifths
vote of the council is required.  As matters currently stand,

Fair Political Practices
Commission                          Page - 2                    April 30, 1985


it is not possible to get such a vote.  Is this not a situation
qualifying for special consideration under Government Code
Section 87101, which would permit the developer-councilmember
to vote?

Your immediate attention to this matter would be most
appreciated.

                                    Yours truly,

                                    McFALL, BURNETT & BRINTON

                                    [signature]
                                    John A. Frederick
                                    Attorney at Law

JAF/bv

cc:  David Jinkens, City Manager

LAW OFFICES OF

HYDE, MILLER & SAVAGE

TIMOTHY E.ANSWORTH
JEFFREY A.DELANO
RICHARD M.HYDE*
NANCY C.MILLER
CHRISTINA PRIM
LEE SAVAGE

428 J STREET, SUITE 400
SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
(916) 447-7933

*A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION

May 7, 1985


Ms. Diane Fishburn, Esquire
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, CA  95814

Dear Diane:

    This letter will confirm our telephone conversation of May
1, 1985.  I requested an oral opinion of the FPPC as to how one
would proceed given the following hypothetical facts.

    Pursuant to Section 84308, members of a public agency have
received contributions of $250 or more from a "party" as defined,
within the previous twelve (12) months, prohibiting such members
from participating, influencing or otherwise commenting upon a
proposed contract with the party.  If this resulted in an
insufficient quorum to award the contract to the "party", you
indicated that Regulation 18701 would apply and a "rule of
necessity" could be invoked.  You further referred me to the
Hudson Decision for further guidance (4 FPPC Opinions 13).

                                    Very truly yours,

                                    HYDE, MILLER AND SAVAGE

                                    By:[illegible]
                                       NANCY C. MILLER

     Due to a recent sewage problem, our council adopted an

     Our city is now confronted with a problem - to stop the

sections.question: Is this a situation qualifying for special consideration under Government Code section 87101, which would permit the developer-councilmember to vote?
sections.conclusion: If it was absolutely necessary for them to seek a four-vote decision on the extension of the interim ordinance, the councilmember in question could participate without fear of enforcement action on the part of the FPPC. The basis for this advice is the application of 87101 and 18701 -- legally required participation -- to the circumstances set forth in the attached letter and the fact that the hospitalized councilmember had a heart attack and her doctor will not permit her to participate in any way whatsoever.
sections.question_synthetic: Can a city councilmember with a conflict of interest (real estate developer subject to building moratorium) vote on extending a building moratorium ordinance when a four-fifths vote is required and another councilmember is hospitalized?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC advised that the conflicted councilmember could participate in the vote if absolutely necessary, citing Government Code sections 87101 and 18701 (legally required participation rule) given that the other hospitalized councilmember could not participate.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains multiple related letters from April-May 1985 regarding the same matter. Main advice is contained in May 20, 1985 memorandum from Bob Leidigh. The requestor accepted informal assistance in lieu of formal advice letter. One supplemental letter discusses a hypothetical situation regarding Section 84308 contributions and Regulation 18701.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Is this a situation qualifying for special consideration under Government Code section 87101, which would permit the developer-councilmember to vote?

CONCLUSION: If it was absolutely necessary for them to seek a four-vote decision on the extension of the interim ordinance, the councilmember in question could participate without fear of enforcement action on the part of the FPPC. The basis for this advice is the application of 87101 and 18701 -- legally required participation -- to the circumstances set forth in the attached letter and the fact that the hospitalized councilmember had a heart attack and her doctor will not permit her to participate in any way whatsoever.

He will proceed in that fashion only if it is absolutely necessary to take that step. He will accept this informal assistance in lieu of an advice letter with the understanding that I would have this memorandum prepared and a copy transmitted to tne Enforcement Division. The basis for this advice is the application of 87101 and 18701 -- legally required participation -- to the circumstances set forth in the attached letter and the fact that the hospitalized councilmember had a heart attack and her doctor will not permit her to participate in any way whatsoever. Milman General Counsel BAM:plh CITY ATTORNEY 146 North Grant Street Manteca, California 95336 209-823-1112 [illegible] April 30, 1985 Fair Political Practices Commission 1100 K Street Sacramento, CA 95814 Re: Building Moratorium Our File No. 226-85-202 Dear Sir/Madam: This office is the contract city attorney for the City of Manteca, a general law city. Our governing body consists of five councilmembers, including a mayor who has a vote on the council equal to the other four members. Due to a recent sewage problem, our council adopted an urgency ordinance (No. 710) pursuant to Government Code Section 65858, which ordinance placed a halt on the issuance of all building permits requiring sewer connections. To maintain the moratorium until a non-emergency ordinance and allocation system can be adopted, it is necessary to extend Ordinance No. 710 beyond the initial forty-five day period provided for in § 65858. Pursuant to subdivision (a) of § 65858 any extension of Ordinance No. 710 requires a four-fifths affirmative vote of the council.
embedding.summary: FPPC provided informal advice to the City of Manteca regarding whether a real estate developer-councilmember could vote on a building moratorium extension ordinance when another councilmember was hospitalized and a four-fifths vote was required. The FPPC advised that the conflicted councilmember could vote if absolutely necessary under the legally required participation doctrine.
